title: Peter Pan
author: After J.M. Barrie
paragraphs: On a quiet street in London, there stood a cozy little house where the Darling family lived. Inside, three children shared a nursery on the top floor: Wendy, the eldest, who loved to tell stories; John, who wore his father's old spectacles and fancied himself very grown-up; and little Michael, who went nowhere without his stuffed bear.

Every night, their mother would tuck them in one by one, kiss their foreheads, and dim the nightlight to a soft golden glow. The nursery window looked out over the rooftops of London, and on clear evenings the children could see the stars winking at them, as though the sky itself were telling a secret.

One night, the moon hung especially low and bright. A strange shadow slipped through the window and danced across the ceiling. It leaped over the toy chest, skipped along the wall, and somersaulted onto the floor. And then, standing on the windowsill with the whole city glittering behind him, appeared a boy dressed in leaves and moonlight.

His name was Peter Pan, and he had never grown up, not even a little bit. Beside him hovered a tiny point of light that jingled like a silver bell whenever it moved. This was Tinker Bell, a fairy no bigger than your hand, who left a trail of golden dust wherever she flew.

Peter grinned at the three children, who sat up in their beds with wide eyes and open mouths. He asked them a question that changed everything: would they like to learn how to fly? Wendy clasped her hands together. John straightened his spectacles. Michael squeezed his bear tight and nodded so hard his whole body shook.

The secret, Peter explained, was to think of the happiest thing you could imagine, and then to let Tinker Bell sprinkle you with fairy dust. The little fairy circled around each child, and shimmering golden powder drifted down onto their hair, their shoulders, and their pajamas like warm, glowing snow.

Wendy thought of summer afternoons reading under the apple tree. John thought of Christmas morning and the crackle of wrapping paper. Michael thought of his mother's arms around him, warm and safe. And one by one, their toes lifted off the floorboards, and they floated gently upward, as light as soap bubbles in a breeze.

Out through the nursery window they soared, over chimney pots breathing thin ribbons of smoke, over the glowing streetlamps, over the dark ribbon of the Thames. London grew small beneath them. The night sky opened wide, vast and soft, and the stars seemed close enough to touch if you only reached out your hand.

Peter pointed the way. Second star to the right, he said, and straight on till morning. They sailed through clouds that felt cool and damp against their cheeks, like the mist that rolls in from the sea. They flew for what felt like hours or perhaps only minutes, because time moves strangely when you are between the stars.

And then, rising out of the darkness below, an island appeared. It was ringed with silver beaches and draped in emerald jungle. Waterfalls tumbled from mossy cliffs into lagoons that shimmered turquoise and violet. Rainbows arched from peak to peak. This was Neverland, the place where no one ever had to grow old.

In the lagoon, mermaids sat on smooth rocks, combing their long hair and singing songs that had no words, only feelings. Their voices drifted across the water like a lullaby made of moonlight. Wendy waved to them, and one mermaid smiled and slipped beneath the surface with barely a ripple.

Deep in the forest, beneath the roots of an enormous old tree, lived the Lost Boys. They were children who had tumbled out of their prams long ago and were never claimed, so they ended up here. Their underground home was snug and warm, lit by candles stuck into the walls, with hammocks strung from root to root.

The Lost Boys gathered around Wendy with hopeful eyes. Would she be their mother, they asked, and tell them bedtime stories? Wendy smiled, tucked the smallest boy into his hammock, and began to tell a story in her soft, steady voice. The boys listened with their eyes half-closed, and one by one they drifted off to sleep.

Of course, Neverland also had its dangers. Captain Hook prowled the waters in his dark ship, its black sails blotting out the stars. He had a gleaming iron hook where his left hand used to be, lost long ago to a crocodile. That very crocodile had swallowed a ticking clock, so wherever it went you could hear it coming: tick, tock, tick, tock.

Peter and the children outwitted the pirates at every turn, swinging through the treetops, hiding behind the mermaids' rocks, and laughing so hard that the whole island seemed to laugh with them. Every day brought a new adventure, and every night the stars of Neverland watched over them as they slept.

But one evening, as the sky turned from rose to violet, Wendy looked up at the stars and thought of another window, far away in London, still open, still waiting. She thought of her mother sitting in the rocking chair beside it, and a gentle ache settled in her chest like a warm stone.

She told her brothers it was time to go home. John nodded quietly. Michael pressed his bear against his cheek. Peter said nothing at all. He only looked out toward the horizon, the moonlight resting on his face, which would never change, not ever.

Peter flew them back across the sea of stars, through the cool clouds, over the sleeping city. The nursery window was still open, just as they had left it, and warm golden light spilled out into the night. Their mother was there, waiting, just as Wendy had known she would be.

The children floated down to the nursery floor and tumbled into their mother's arms. The blankets were still warm. The pillows were still soft. Everything was just as it had been, except that Wendy's hair still glittered faintly with specks of golden fairy dust.

Wendy closed her eyes and smiled, and sleep came to her as gently as a feather settling on still water. The window stayed open, the curtain stirring in the night breeze. Far away, a tiny point of light streaked across the stars. Perhaps it was Tinker Bell. Perhaps it was Peter Pan. Perhaps it was only a shooting star. But the window would always be left open, just for him.
